narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 96% chance that you would not pass based on key aspects of your academic record. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger profiles for bar success. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your third-year grade decile, which placed you in the bottom tier of your school and strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure compared to the much higher deciles—around the sixth—typical for those who passed. Your LSAT score of 24 also played a major role in pushing the prediction toward failure, as it falls well below the average of about 37 seen among successful bar takers. Even your first-year grade decile, also at the bottom, contributed to this outlook, though with somewhat less weight than the others. These three academic indicators were the top influences, all pointing in the direction of potential struggle on the bar rather than success. While factors like your middle family income bracket align with over a third of those who passed, and your choice to study full-time matches nearly all successful students, they weren't enough to offset the concerns from your grades and test score. In contrast, admitted students typically show much stronger early and later academic performance, with LSATs in the mid-to-high range that build confidence in bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where these metrics strongly predicted outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the 37 average, strengthening your undergraduate record if possible through additional coursework, or exploring post-baccalaureate programs to improve your decile rankings. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or apply to schools with more flexible admission criteria while building these areas. With focused effort on these academic standouts, you can reshape your profile for better odds next time.
